parts.answer: What does the conductor think he has seen ahead on the railway line (line 2)? Another train/red lights of a train. Why is Fred concerned by what the conductor says (line 3)? See 1(b)(ii). What does Fred think has caused the conductor to say this? • he conductor is becoming unreliable (doesn’t trust the conductor)/says he has seen something ahead (when there obviously isn’t)/because Fred has seen nothing • Fred thinks the conductor is hallucinating/imagining what he sees. • Fred thinks conductor has lost confidence in Fred/is afraid of storm. (Note: 1 mark for each point to a maximum of 2. Candidates may make these points under either (i) or (ii), but may be awarded only 1 mark for (i) and 1 mark for (ii)).

Give two details about the station master’s reaction to Fred that show he does not agree with Fred’s suggestion (paragraph two): • He has a ‘frown’. • He shakes his head. 1 mark for each to a maximum of 2

Using your own words, explain what you understand by the phrase: ‘«his face lacerated by the gusts«’ (line 26). His face felt as if it had been slashed/injured/cut by the strength of the wind and snow. 1 mark for a partial explanation, 2 marks for a full explanation in own words.

State two expressions which suggest that Fred finds driving the train painful (paragraph 6). • His face is ‘lacerated’. • He ‘suffers terribly’. 1 mark for each to a maximum of 2 October/November Question Answer Marks

State two reactions of Mr Beeching that suggest he does not want to help Fred (lines 34–39). • He says he has done his job/has given Fred a warning • He says “Get going!”/Tells Fred they mustn’t annoy the passengers. • Is silent • Gives signal himself 1 mark for each to a maximum of 2 Re-read paragraph five. Explain, using your own words, what the writer means by the word in italics in each of the following phrases: (a) ‘«the train was struggling in the immense white whirlwind’ (lines 17–18) Powerful storm/gale/hurricane/twisted violently by strong wind (b) ‘...in this uncertain light nothing could be distinguished.’ (line 18) Seen clearly/differentiated (c) ‘«this wilderness of snow.’ (lines 20–21) Desolate place/middle of nowhere/far from civilisation Explain how the language in each of the phrases in Question (g)(i) helps to suggest the harshness of the weather conditions. (a) The train is being over powered/being prevented from moving by a huge and violent storm. [2] (b) It is becoming dark and it is getting harder to see anything clearly. [2] (c) The snow is thick/heavy and covers everything make it feel unfamiliar like a remote place. [2] Award 1 mark for a partial explanation of each phrase. Award 2 marks for an explanation of each phrase which shows clear appreciation of the effect of the writer’s use of language. Paraphrase of chosen phrase = 0 for explanation. Explanation must also be predominantly in candidate’s own words. (Note: the definitions above for both part (i) and part (ii) contain the essence of an answer. Be careful not to credit a word actually used in the quoted phrase. However, accept that candidates may respond in different ways, e.g. at greater length.) October/November Question 2 This question tests reading assessment objectives R1 to R3 (10 marks): R1 demonstrate understanding of explicit meanings R2 demonstrate understanding of implicit meanings and attitudes R3 analyse, evaluate and develop facts, ideas and opinions AND writing assessm
